In the original code, wcswidth would repeatedly call wcwidth, leading to a high function call overhead. Having wcwidth call wcswidth with a one-char string instead thus improves performance. The problem is, that the original wcwidth would return -1 for control characters or combining characters, where wcswidth would return -1 if it found a control character and ignore any combining characters (treat them as width 0).

Since there are a lot of recursive `fastdtw()` calls, it would make sense to expose this function in C (`cdef`). You can, instead, make a lightweight entry point function which ensures the arguments are correctly casted (i.e. into typed memoryviews) and then just passes the control flow to an internal recursive function (i.e. once you leave Python, be as much C as possible).
```cython
def fastdtw(x, y, radius=1, dist=lambda a, b: abs(a - b)):
    x = np.asanyarray(x, dtype=float)
    y = np.asanyarray(y, dtype=float)
    return _fastdtw(x, y, radius, dist)

cdef _fastdtw(double[:] x, double[:] y, int radius, dist):
    ...
```
Again, it's much easier and common with NumPy, but memoryviews can also be [constructed from cython.arrays](https://webcache.googleusercontent.com/search?strip=1&q=cache:http://docs.cython.org/src/userguide/memoryviews.html#cython-arrays).

Should I include the two attributes in the `Topology` class? I think it's a bit dirty to just leave the `Star` topology out. The `neighbor_idx` attribute could be useful for future PSO algorithms (e.g. for accessing the neighbours outside the topology). In the `Topology` class it'd look like this:

```python
class Topology(object):
    def __init__(self, static=False, **kwargs):
        """Initializes the class"""

        # Initialize logger
        self.logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

        self.static = static
        self.neighbor_idx = None

        try:
            if self.static:
                cli_print("Running on `dynamic` topology, neighbors are updated regularly."
                          "Set `static=True` for fixed neighbors.",
                          1,
                          0,
                          self.logger)
        except AttributeError:
            pass
```

`USE_X_FORWARDED_PORT` was introduced in django 1.9, and if we're on the `except AttributeError:` path it means we're using an older version so we can't use that setting. In this case, [`get_host()` will respect the `USE_X_FORWARDED_HOST` setting](https://github.com/django/django/blob/stable/1.8.x/django/http/request.py#L75-L86), and the reverse proxy can be configured to pass the port along with the `X-Forwarded-Host` header.

If you prefer we can fallback to using `self.request.META['SERVER_PORT']` from `except IndexError:`. The behaviour would be the same, as the port would just be stripped in the python-saml code later on.

Thank you for giving a direction to think about it.

Let me explain why it is difficult to design it in that way.
Here is an example of overloading constructor in Scala:

```scala
class A(i: Int) {
  \\ Part 1
  val initData = 5
  def this(i: Int, j: Int) = {
    \\ You can't write anything here
    this(i + j)
    \\ Part 3
  }
  \\ Part 2
  def getData() = initData
}
```
If `new A(10)`, Part 1 and Part 2 will be executed.
If `new A(10, 20)`, Part 1, Part 2, and the Part 3 will be executed.
When you initialize fields and methods in Part 1 and Part 2, it's late to do anything in Part 3.
When executing 'common' portion Part 1 and Part 2, we must pass the original version of argument set, which loses the information of custom names for multiple input.

One possible way is declaring the field as `var`.
Since using mutable variable is not good for maintaining program, I don't recommend this way.

Maybe there is an [alternative strategy](https://github.com/Ldpe2G/DeepLearningForFun/blob/master/Mxnet-Scala/MatrixFactorization/matrixFac.scala) to manipulate multiple input. 
It doesn't use `Module` so that avoid using `NDArrayIter`. 
Since `Module` aims to provide high-level API, I think it's worthy to dedicated to `NDArrayIter`.

mmh, it's not about hiding something secret and the mapping is not so straighforward.

**Mapping (more or less):**
HTTPTimeoutError -> twisted.web._newclient.ResponseNeverReceived
ConnectTimeoutError -> twisted.internet.error.ConnectError

**Twisted alternative**
I could just let all Twisted exceptions bubble up, but in that case if you are a Fido user, you are required to also understand Twisted, and open Twisted code to understand what exceptions are thrown in case of connection or timeout issues (the naming in this case is confusing, those would be twisted.web._newclient.ResponseNeverReceived and twisted.internet.error.ConnectError). With this Fido change, the work is done for you.

This abstraction is particularly useful to implement retries at the user-app level. If you want to just retry in case any connection issues or timeout occurred, you could just catch the Base class.

**Why this PR?**
I am actually open to implement any of the two solutions, but **a change in the exception handling and contract (major version bump) is unfortunately required** as in the current implementation there is no distinction between HTTP Timeouts or Connect Errors (they are grouped and hidden) and this is required to implement retries mechanisms with SmastStack.

I think this new comment is not quite correct. The original comment was saying basically that the root span is a `server` span, but the local child spans can be client and/or server. In fact, our "local" spans use both cs/cr and ss/sr annotations.

You can still have this with a client root span. Imagine a application that will download some file, and also has some local method for validation that we instrumented.  We might have

```
cs:          download_file
cs/sr/ss/sr: validate_parameters_locally
cs:          fetch_file_from_server
cr:          fetch_file_from_server
cr:          download_file
```

You could even imagine child server spans in there if somewhere in the middle we check some queue for new data.

Mhm, it stems from [this part of discussion](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/pull/1771#discussion_r71123666). Thinking about the cases of disabling and resources missing, it's still true that the instance remained "pending". (In the common sense common denominator of those more detailed statuses. If we invested more in status propagation we would come up with summaries like "pending because missing resources" etc.)

I'm saying that in my view the higher level conceptual task's flow hasn't changed much and doesn't risk changing much. It's first pending, then becomes running (the moment when `run()` is invoked). The reason for failing running is always "known" via the exception; the reason for not running at all can vary, but "left pending" remains valid wording.

Ideally, each engine would live in its own module, so we would have something like this:
```
-- draftjs_exporter/
   |-- engines/
   |   |-- __init__.py       # Empty
   |   |-- base.py          # With the DOMEngine definition
   |   |-- html5lib.py     # With the DOM_HTML5LIB definition
   |   `-- lxml.py           # With the DOM_LXML definition
   `-- ...
```
And within each engine module, the import would be done normally (i.e. without wrapping them inside a try/except block).

Then, in `draftjs_exporter/dom.py` we would never import the engines directly (i.e. at the top of the file), but rather use the path import from django (see my comment in `draftjs_exporter/dom.py`) so it always only import the relevant engines and dependencies.

With the current implementation, several things could happen:
- None of the dependencies are available (or the dependencies for the engine selected in the DOM object is missing), the import error will be silenced and an AttributeError will be raise when calling `create_tag` on the selected engine (e.g. for the `DOM_HTML5LIB` engine, when `create_tag` is called, you'll get `AttributeError: dom_engine module has no attribute 'soup'` which will be pretty unhelpful),
- Both `BeautifulSoup` and `lxml` are available in the virtual environment and will both be loaded.

With the proposed solution above, only the selected engine will be loaded and it is missing some dependencies, it will raise an `ImportError` which will make it pretty obvious that you are missing dependencies.

@MattiSG,

We need another opinion to cut short this discussion.
We're inside the `new_filled_column` function, which builds an OpenFisca column (=variable) based on what has been coded by the legislation writer (`class rsa(Variable)...`).

The function `get_datetime_date ` parses a `'YYYY-MM-DD'` string and returns the equivalent `datetime.date`.

The suggested implementation here is to:

- First call the `get_datetime_date` function once when building the column, just to check if it returns an error. We do not save the result.
- [Then](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/pull/522/files#diff-31e80408104c8f43c6b796a369a77585R842), a little further, call it for _each_ dated formula to check that its start date is consistent with the variable end date.
- [Finally](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/pull/522/files#diff-31e80408104c8f43c6b796a369a77585R502), we also call it in the `find_function` method, whose job is to find the right dated function to use in a calculation. This method is called _each time_ the variable is calculated.

I think there is no reason to parse a string `1 + k + n` times.
(where`k` = numbers of dated formulas, `n` = numbers of times the variable is calculated). I'm therefore in favour of parsing this string once and for all when we build the variable.

IIUC these should only be accepted for `GoBinary` targets and they should invalidate compiles (installs). There are 2 paths to invalidation:
1. Include field into the default fingerprint of the target. An example is in [`GoLocalSource`], although you just need `add_field` singular + a `PrimitiveField` (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/targets/go_local_source.py#L67)
2. Use a custom `FingerprintStrategy` in the task(s) that cares about this field. A FingerprintEStrategy example is [here](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/contrib/scrooge/src/python/pants/contrib/scrooge/tasks/java_thrift_library_fingerprint_strategy.py#L33) and and you'd pass it in as the custom `fingerprint_strategy` in the `GoCompile` [invalidated block](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/contrib/go/src/python/pants/contrib/go/tasks/go_compile.py#L39).

I think you want 2 here since build flags are only used by `GoCompile` now and conceivably forever. In addition, the fingerprinting probably ought to be over the final `build_flags` you compose in `GoCompile` since order matter in the case of dups. This probably also means taking the `fingerprint=True` off the option (and maybe adding a comment that fp is handled by the custom FPStrategy) and bumping the task `implementation_version`.
Also, I don't think you need the explicit default of `None` in `get(target, None)`: The default is implicitly None if unspecified.

If we define a function with named optional parameters, their order is not fixed, e.g.

```
def start_span(self, operation_name, parent=None, tags=None, start_time=None)
```
only requires operation to be the first arg, and even that only if you're passing it as positional, passing by name can have any order.
